I agree with the prior conclusions that there is a genetic cause of craniostenosis. The evidence shows that over 86% of cases involve single-gene mutations, with key genes in the FGFR pathway (e.g., FGFR1–3) and others like SMAD6, TWIST1, TCF12, ERF, and MSX2 being strongly implicated, confirming a clear molecular-genetic foundation even in non-syndromic cases. \boxed{A} Based on the latest available evidence, the majority of craniostenosis cases—including both syndromic and non-syndromic subtypes—are attributable to identifiable genetic mutations, making

I agree with the prior conclusions that there is a genetic cause of craniostenosis. The evidence shows that over 86% of cases involve single-gene mutations, with key genes in the FGFR pathway (e.g., FGFR1–3) and others like SMAD6, TWIST1, TCF12, ERF, and MSX2 being strongly implicated, confirming a clear molecular-genetic foundation even in non-syndromic cases. \boxed{A} Based on the latest available evidence, the majority of craniostenosis cases—including both syndromic and non-syndromic subtypes—are attributable to identifiable genetic mutations, making option A correct.
